summary: The paper revisits translation-equivariant neural processes (NPs) and offers a more principled and interpretable route to constructing them. Its first contribution is a characterization of continuous translation-equivariant operators via the Volterra expansion, which expresses such operators as a sum of higher-order convolutions and thereby makes the induced function class analytically transparent while suggesting an efficient first-order approximation. Its second contribution is the set Fourier convolution (SFConv), a frequency-domain parameterization that applies the continuous Fourier transform directly to irregularly sampled points, avoiding spatial discretization, approximating a global receptive field, and scaling linearly in the number of observations. Building on these ideas, the authors instantiate two conditional NP families: SFConvCNP, which stacks SFConv blocks interleaved with pointwise non-linearities, and SFVConvCNP, which incorporates the Volterra formulation directly. Experiments on 1D/2D/3D synthetic and real-world regression benchmarks show competitive or improved performance against strong translation-equivariant baselines, together with ablations on the method's key hyperparameters.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well motivated and technically coherent. It opens from two concrete and recognizable limitations of existing translation-equivariant NPs — the opacity of the induced function class when generic components are stacked with non-linearities, and the scalability constraints of convolutional designs (local kernels, dense uniform grids) versus quadratic attention — and the proposed machinery is designed to address both. On soundness, the technical development is careful. The Volterra characterization gives an analytically transparent view of translation-equivariant operators, and the central SFConv construction — taking the Fourier transform of the convolutional deep set so as to operate directly on scattered points rather than a grid — is an elegant idea that cleanly resolves the grid-mismatch problem while retaining linear complexity. The continuous-transform derivation and the spectral weighting scheme rest on solid mathematical grounding, and the resulting architectural motif (interleaving spectral/set convolutions with pointwise non-linearities) is a natural one, echoing transformers, message-passing GNNs, and Fourier neural operators. Empirically the work is well supported: it evaluates across 1D/2D/3D tasks, is competitive with or better than strong baselines, and includes sensible ablations — in particular the frequency-grid ablation that fixes the total grid size to separate spectral coverage from resolution and isolate the role of that hyperparameter. The appendices are thorough and give full experimental detail.

On originality, the individual ingredients are not all new in isolation, and the novelty lies more in the combination and in the operator-level characterization than in any single component; nonetheless the synthesis is meaningful and the Fourier-of-deep-set construction is a genuinely neat contribution.

The main weaknesses concern presentation of the main text and the scope of the evaluation. On presentation, while the writing is generally clear, the main text does not foreground the contributions as well as it could: the abstract is long, Sections 3.1–3.2 read as further background and blur the boundary between what is already established and the paper's own methodological moves, and Section 3.3 — which introduces the contributed architectures — underspecifies them in the main text, deferring detail to the appendix without clear pointers. The central derivations (Eqns 12–14) are delivered as technical narration rather than highlighted, lemma-style statements with explicit assumptions, which makes their role harder to parse. Relatedly, the “approximately global receptive field” is presented as a headline property but is not convincingly demonstrated; as with CNNs, the theoretical receptive field need not match the effective one, so this claim deserves more direct theoretical and empirical support.

On significance and evaluation, the experiments, while sound, are somewhat light in volume and complexity: the tasks are relatively simple and the range is limited, which is the weakest aspect of the paper. Two comparisons in particular would strengthen the case. First, the closest cousins of this approach are FNO-based / spectral NPs (e.g., the spectral convolutional CNP), which are mentioned only in passing; given how central this line is, it deserves a more prominent discussion and inclusion in the tables and experiments, ideally probing whether the proposed models inherit the known FNO difficulty on problems with rich, non-band-limited spectral structure. Second, the scalability narrative would be sharpened by positioning against efficient/linear-attention variants and sub-quadratic transformer-NP variants rather than only vanilla quadratic attention. The evaluation could also be broadened with probabilistic calibration metrics (coverage probability and/or CRPS) and simpler reference baselines such as a stationary/non-stationary GP, which would clarify when the added machinery is worthwhile. Finally, qualitative figures — an architecture/data-flow schematic and visualizations of regression fits and spectral effects — are largely absent and would aid readability, and the paper's own weaknesses and the scope of its claims are not discussed explicitly. Overall, these are real but largely addressable issues, and the combination of a principled construction, sound technical tools, and solid empirical results makes this a worthwhile contribution.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. The approximately global receptive field is presented as a headline property. Can you substantiate it more directly, both theoretically and empirically, given that effective receptive fields can differ substantially from theoretical ones (as they do for CNNs)?

2. Can you sharpen the relationship to FNO-/spectral-based NPs (e.g., the spectral convolutional CNP), discuss it more centrally, and include such models in the tables and experiments? In particular, do SFConvCNP/SFVConvCNP inherit the known FNO difficulty on problems with rich, non-band-limited spectral structure?

3. How does performance behave as the frequency grid |Ξ| grows (capacity scaling, and any signs of under- or overfitting), and how sensitive is training to the Gaussian kernel length-scale initialization when the initial bandwidth is mismatched to the local sampling density? Does the SFConv block correspond exactly to the first-order term of the Volterra expansion?

4. Could you add probabilistic calibration metrics (e.g., coverage probability and/or CRPS) and simpler reference baselines such as a (stationary and non-stationary) GP, and clarify in which regimes the proposed models are preferable to such simpler approaches?

5. Could Sections 3.1–3.3 be reorganized to separate established results from your methodological contributions, and could the central derivations (Eqns 12–14) be elevated to lemma-style statements with explicit assumptions?
limitations: The limitations discussion is thin and would benefit from being more explicit. In particular, the paper should acknowledge the gap between its stated properties and what is actually demonstrated — notably the approximately global receptive field and the claimed deterioration of attention-based baselines under translation — and delineate the regimes where the approach may be less favorable, such as problems with complex spectral structure or settings where a simple GP already suffices.
